q: The molarity of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sample which has density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and mass percentage of <math><mn>63</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> is ________ (Molecular Weight of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>63</mn></math> )
question: The molarity of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sample which has density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and mass percentage of <math><mn>63</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> is ________ (Molecular Weight of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>63</mn></math> )
solution:
63%ww⟶HNO3 solution
M=63×1.463×100×1000mole/L
M=14mole/L

subject: Chemistry
q: Among $10^{-9} \mathrm{~g}$ (each) of the following elements, which one will have the highest number of atoms? Element : $\mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Po}, \mathrm{Pr}$ and Pt
question: Among $10^{-9} \mathrm{~g}$ (each) of the following elements, which one will have the highest number of atoms? Element : $\mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Po}, \mathrm{Pr}$ and Pt
solution:
$\text { No. of atoms }=\frac{\text { Massin } g}{\operatorname{Molar} \operatorname{Mas}(\mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
Therefore for the same Mass element having the least Molar mass will have the higher no. of atoms.
- $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Po}}=209$
- $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Pr}}=141$
- $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Pb}}=207$
- $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Pt}}=195$

subject: Chemistry
q: The complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound containing '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>', '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>' gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>793</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>442</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The percentage of oxygen composition in the organic compound is____.(nearest integer)
question: The complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound containing '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>', '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>' gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>793</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>442</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The percentage of oxygen composition in the organic compound is____.(nearest integer)
solution:
Organic compound +O2⟶  CO2g     +    H2O                                                          0.793 gram       0.442 gram

Weight of the organic compound=0.492 gram

Number of moles of CO2=Number of moles of C=0.79344Weight of carbon=0.7934412=0.216g

Weight of hydrogen=0.442182=0.0491g

Weight of oxygen=0.492-0.216-0.0491=0.2269g

% of O=0.22490.492×100=46.11≈46

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reaction:

3PbCl2+2NH43PO4→Pb3PO42+6NH4Cl

If 72 mmol PbCl2 is mixed with 50 mmol of NH43PO4, then amount of Pb3PO42 formed in mmol. (nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following reaction:

3PbCl2+2NH43PO4→Pb3PO42+6NH4Cl

If 72 mmol PbCl2 is mixed with 50 mmol of NH43PO4, then amount of Pb3PO42 formed in mmol. (nearest integer)
solution:
The reaction is given as,

3PbCl2+2NH43PO4→Pb3PO42+6NH4Cl

The products formed in the above reaction is Pb3PO42 and NH4Cl  .

According to the above stoichiometric equation, three mole of each lead chloride and two moles of NH43PO4 is required to make one mole of Pb3PO42 and 6 moles of NH4Cl   

Here the Limiting Reagent is PbCl2

mmol of Pb3(PO4)2 formed

=mmol of PbCl2 reacted 3=723

=24 m mol

subject: Chemistry
q: Hemoglobin contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of iron by mass. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hemoglobin is (Given : Atomic mass of Fe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> in<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>022</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Hemoglobin contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of iron by mass. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hemoglobin is (Given : Atomic mass of Fe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> in<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>022</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Mass of haemoglobin = 3.3g

Mass of iron atoms = 0.34100×3.3=0.01122g

56 g of iron contains = 1 mole of iron or 6.023 × 1023 atoms

Number of moles of atoms=Mass atomic mass

Number of moles of 0.01122g of iron is

Number of moles = 0.01122g56g=0.00020 moles

Number of atoms =Number of moles ×avogadro number

No. of Fe atoms

 =0.00020×6.022×1023=1.2046 × 1020

subject: Chemistry
q:
The molality of a 10%(v/V) solution of di-bromine solution in CCl4 (carbon tetrachloride) is x '. x= _____×10-2M. (Nearest integer)

[Given : molar mass of Br2=160 g mol-1

atomic mass of C=12 g mol-1

atomic mass of Cl=35.5 g mol-1

density of dibromine =3.2 g cm-3

density of CCl4=1.6 g cm-3
question:
The molality of a 10%(v/V) solution of di-bromine solution in CCl4 (carbon tetrachloride) is x '. x= _____×10-2M. (Nearest integer)

[Given : molar mass of Br2=160 g mol-1

atomic mass of C=12 g mol-1

atomic mass of Cl=35.5 g mol-1

density of dibromine =3.2 g cm-3

density of CCl4=1.6 g cm-3
solution:
10% v/V means 10 ml solute in 90 ml solvent.

mass of soluteBr2 =10×3.2=32g

mass of solventCCl4 =90×1.6 g

molality(m) =mass of solutemolar mass of solute×1000mass of solvent

m=32×1000160×90×1.6=1.388

m=138.8×10-2=139

question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is dissolved in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. Mole fraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> in solution and molality (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>mol</mi><mtext> </mtext><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) of the solution respectively are:
solution:

n2=1818=1;
nt=n1+n2=0.2+1=1.2

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound (X) with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math> carbon will produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on complete combustion.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound (X) with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math> carbon will produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on complete combustion.
solution:
To calculate the mass percent of an element in a compound, we divide the mass of the element in 1 mole of the compound by the compound's molar mass and multiply the result by 100.

The combustion reaction is Cs+O2g→CO2g

C%=1244× Wt. of CO2 Wt. of organic compound ×100

60=1244× Wt. of CO20.5×100

Wt. of CO2=1.1

subject: Chemistry
q: The amount of $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ formed upon mixing 100 $\mathrm{mL}$ of $20.8 \% \mathrm{BaCl}_2$ solution with $50 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $9.8 \%$ $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ solution will be: $(\mathrm{Ba}=137, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{~S}=32, \mathrm{H}=1$ and $\mathrm{O}=16$ )
question: The amount of $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ formed upon mixing 100 $\mathrm{mL}$ of $20.8 \% \mathrm{BaCl}_2$ solution with $50 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $9.8 \%$ $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ solution will be: $(\mathrm{Ba}=137, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{~S}=32, \mathrm{H}=1$ and $\mathrm{O}=16$ )
solution:
$\mathrm{BaCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaSO}_4+2 \mathrm{HCl}$
$\begin{array}{llll}208 \mathrm{~g} & 98 \mathrm{~g} & 233 \mathrm{~g} & 73 \mathrm{~g} \\ 20.8 \mathrm{~g} & 9.8 \mathrm{~g} & 23.3 \mathrm{~g} & 7.3 \mathrm{~g}\end{array}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The amount of arsenic pentasulphide that can be obtained when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> arsenic acid is treated with excess <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> in the presence of conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> (assuming <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> conversion) is
question: The amount of arsenic pentasulphide that can be obtained when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> arsenic acid is treated with excess <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> in the presence of conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> (assuming <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> conversion) is
solution:
2H3AsO4+5H2S→Conc.HClAs2S5+8H2O

2 moles of Arsenic Acid → 1 mole of Arsenic Pentasulphide

1 moles of Arsenic Acid → 1/2 mole of Arsenic Pentasulphide

Molar mass of H3AsO4=141; Molar mass of As2S5=308

∴ Number of moles of H3AsO4=35.5141=0.25

∴  Number of moles of As2S5=0.252=0.125 mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
When 81.0 g of aluminium is allowed to react with 128.0 g of oxygen gas, the mass of aluminium oxide produced in grams is_______ - (Nearest integer)
Given :
Molar mass of Al is $27.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of O is $16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
question:
When 81.0 g of aluminium is allowed to react with 128.0 g of oxygen gas, the mass of aluminium oxide produced in grams is_______ - (Nearest integer)
Given :
Molar mass of Al is $27.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of O is $16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \\ & \frac{81}{27}=3 \text { mole } \frac{128}{32}=4 \mathrm{~mole}\end{aligned}$


Limiting reagent
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { mole of } \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \text { formed }=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \text { mole } \\
& \therefore \text { wt. of } \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \text { formed }=\frac{3}{2} \times 102 \\
& \quad=153 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Production of iron in blast furnace follows the following equation

Fe3O4s+4COg→3Fel+4CO2g

when 4.640kg of Fe3O4 and 2.520kg of CO are allowed to react then the amount of iror (in g) produced is :

[Given: Molar Atomic mass gmol-1:Fe=56

Molar Atomic mass gmolm-1:O=16

Molar Atomic mass gmolm-1:C=12]
question:
Production of iron in blast furnace follows the following equation

Fe3O4s+4COg→3Fel+4CO2g

when 4.640kg of Fe3O4 and 2.520kg of CO are allowed to react then the amount of iror (in g) produced is :

[Given: Molar Atomic mass gmol-1:Fe=56

Molar Atomic mass gmolm-1:O=16

Molar Atomic mass gmolm-1:C=12]
solution:
Moles of Fe3O4=4.640×103232=20

Moles of CO=2.52×10328=90

So limiting Reagent =Fe3O4

So moles of Fe formed =60

Weight of Fe=60×56=3360gms

subject: Chemistry
q: 20 mL of 2 M NaOH solution is added to 400 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution. The final concentration of the solution is _______ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest integer)
question: 20 mL of 2 M NaOH solution is added to 400 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution. The final concentration of the solution is _______ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest integer)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & M_F=\frac{M_1 V_1+M_2 V_2}{V_1+V_2} \\ & =\frac{2 \times 20+0.5 \times 400}{420}=0.571 \mathrm{M} \\ & =57.1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \\ & =57\end{aligned}$

solution:

=99.68 V

question:

subject: Chemistry
q: For the following reaction, the mass of water produced from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>445</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>57</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>110</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>57</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>110</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>163</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mn>114</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>110</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>l</mn></mfenced></math>
question: For the following reaction, the mass of water produced from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>445</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>57</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>110</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>57</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>110</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>163</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mn>114</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>110</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>l</mn></mfenced></math>
solution:
C57H110O6+1632O2→57CO2+55H2O

Molecular weight of C57H110O6=890

Moles of C57H110O6=weightgmMolecular weight

=445890=12

According to stoichiometry

∵1 mole  of C57H110O6 gives =55 mole of H2O

∵12 mole  of C57H110O6 gives =55×12 mole of H2O

Wt. of H2O= mole of H2O×M Wt. of H2O

=12×55×18=495 gm

subject: Chemistry
q: A commercially sold conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>%</mo><mo> </mo><mi>HCl</mi></math> by mass. If the density of this commercial acid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mL</mi></math>, the molarity of this solution is : (Atomic mass : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>amu</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>amu</mi></math>)
question: A commercially sold conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>%</mo><mo> </mo><mi>HCl</mi></math> by mass. If the density of this commercial acid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mL</mi></math>, the molarity of this solution is : (Atomic mass : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>amu</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>amu</mi></math>)
solution:
Molarity M=x×d×10mB

=35×1.46×1036.5=14 M

mB= molecular mass of solute

d= density of the solution in g/L

x=% solute by mass.

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of the mass percentages of  '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>' of a saturated acyclic organic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> respectively. Then, the moles of oxygen gas required for complete combustion of two moles of organic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is_______
question: The ratio of the mass percentages of  '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>' of a saturated acyclic organic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> respectively. Then, the moles of oxygen gas required for complete combustion of two moles of organic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is_______
solution:
Mass ratio of C : H is 4 : 1⇒12 : 3 

& C : O is 3 : 4 ⇒ 12 : 16 

so         mass    mole    mole ratioC       12          1        1H       3            3        3O      16           1        1

Empirical formula ⇒CH3O

as compound is saturated acyclic so molecular formula is C2H6O2.

C2H6O2+52O2(g)⟶2CO2(g)+3H2O(g)
 2mole       5mole

so required moles of O2 is ⇒5

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of atoms in the following samples of substances is largest in:
question: Number of atoms in the following samples of substances is largest in:
solution:
$4 \mathrm{~g}$ of hydrogen $=4$ mole of hydrogen $=4 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 71.0 \mathrm{gm} \text { of chlorine }=\frac{71.0}{71.0}=1 \text { moles of } \\
& \text { chlorine } \\
& 127 \mathrm{gm} \text { of iodine }=\frac{1}{2} \text { mole of }_2 \\
& =\frac{127}{254}=6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 48.0 \mathrm{gm} \text { of magnesium } \\
& =\frac{48.0}{24.0}=2 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \times \frac{1}{2} \text { atoms } \\
& \therefore 4.0 \mathrm{gm} \mathrm{H}_2 \text { has largest number of atoms. }
\end{aligned}
$$

q:
For a reaction,
N2g+3H2(g)⟶2NH3(g), identify di-hydrogen (H2) as a limiting reagent in the following reaction mixtures.
question:
For a reaction,
N2g+3H2(g)⟶2NH3(g), identify di-hydrogen (H2) as a limiting reagent in the following reaction mixtures.
options: <math><mn>28</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mn>35</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mn>56</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mn>14</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
N2g+3H2g→2NH3g


moles of N2stoichiometry of N2≠mole of H2stoichiometry of H2
Whose ratio is less called limiting reagent.


(a) mole of N2=2828=1
molesstiochiometry ratio of N2=11=1
mole of H2=62=3
molesstiochiometry ratio of H2=33=1
there is no limiting reagent.


(b) mole of N2=3528=1.25
molesstiochiometry ratio of N2=1.251 limiting reagent
mole of H2=82=4
molesstiochiometry ratio of H2=43=1.33


(c) mole of N2=5628=2
molesstiochiometry ratio of N2=21=2
mole of H2=102=5
molesstiochiometry ratio of H2=53=1.66 limiting reagent.


(d) mole of N2=1428=0.5
molesstiochiometry ratio of N2=0.51=0.5 limiting reagent
mole of H2=42=2
molesstiochiometry ratio of H2=23=1.66

∴  Answer is 3 .

subject: Chemistry
q: Dissolving 120 g of a compound of (mol. wt. 60) in 1000 g of water gave a solution of density 1.12 g/mL. The molarity of the solution is :
question: Dissolving 120 g of a compound of (mol. wt. 60) in 1000 g of water gave a solution of density 1.12 g/mL. The molarity of the solution is :
solution:
Mass of compound  =120g ;   moles= 120g 60g/mole =2

Mass of solution  = 1 0 0 0 + 1 2 0 = 1 1 2 0 g

Volume of solution  mass d = 1120g 1.12g/mL =1000mL

Molarity  = 2  moles 1 0 0 0  mL × 1 0 0 0 mL

              = 2 M (no of moles/Litre)

Initial  moleFinal moleN2g1000   +    3H2g500500−300  →  2NH3gLimiting reagent is N2200

subject: Chemistry
q: On complete combustion, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>792</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> of carbon in the organic compound is (Nearest integer)
question: On complete combustion, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>792</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> of carbon in the organic compound is (Nearest integer)
solution:
44 grams of CO2 contain 12 grams of carbon.

Hence, one gram of carbon dioxide contain 1244 gram of carbon.

0.792 gram of carbon dioxide contain 1244 × 0.792 gram of carbon

Percentage of carbon = Mass of carbonMass of organic compound × 100

= 1244 × 0.7920.492 ×100

= 43.90%

So, answer is 44.

subject: Chemistry
q: When a hydrocarbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> undergoes complete combustion it requires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> equivalents of oxygen and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> equivalents of water. What is the molecular formula of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>?
question: When a hydrocarbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> undergoes complete combustion it requires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> equivalents of oxygen and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> equivalents of water. What is the molecular formula of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>?
solution:
By using the formula of combustion analysis:

CxHy+x+y4O2→CO2+y2H2O

Given, x+y4a=11a

x+y4=11

y2a=4a

y=8,

then x+84=11

x = 11-84

x=9

Formula =C9H8

subject: Chemistry
q: What would be the molality of 20% (mass/mass) aqueous solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> ? (molar mass of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>166</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: What would be the molality of 20% (mass/mass) aqueous solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> ? (molar mass of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>166</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
Molality =Moles of soluteweight of solventg×1000
100 gm of solution contains 20 gm KI
So, the moles of KI =20166
Weight of solvent =100-20=80 gm
Molality =20×1000166×80
=1.51 Molal

q: On complete combustion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon dioxide and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The percentage of carbon in the given organic compound is____(Nearest Integer)
question: On complete combustion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon dioxide and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The percentage of carbon in the given organic compound is____(Nearest Integer)
solution:
CxHyOz+x+y4-z2O2→xCO2+y2H2O0.3 g                                     0.2 g      0.1 g

CO2nH2On=xy2=0.2440.118=911

x=9y22

Now, CxHyO2nCO2n=1x

⇒0.312x+y+16z×440.2=1x

66x = 12 x + y + 16 z 54x = y + 16 z

54×9y22 – y = 16 z

z = 29y22

CxHyOz =C9y22HyO29y22= C9H22O29

% of C = 12×912 ×9 +22 +29× 16×100 = 18.18%

subject: Chemistry
q: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound provides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>420</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in organic compound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and ______ respectively. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
question: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound provides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>420</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in organic compound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and ______ respectively. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
solution:
44 gm CO2 have 12 gm carbon

So, 420 gm CO2⇒1244×420

⇒126011gm carbon

⇒114.545 gram carbon

So, % of carbon =114.545750×100

≃15.3%

18 gm H2O⇒2 gm H2

210 gm⇒218×210

=23.33 gm H2

So, % H2⇒23.33750×100=3.11%

≈3%

q:
A 100 mL solution was made by adding 1.43 g of Na2CO3.xH2O. The normality of the solution is 0.1 N. The value of x is _______

(The atomic mass of Na is 23g/mol)
question:
A 100 mL solution was made by adding 1.43 g of Na2CO3.xH2O. The normality of the solution is 0.1 N. The value of x is _______

(The atomic mass of Na is 23g/mol)
solution:
Equivalent of solute =0.1×0.1

Mole of solute Na2CO3,xH2O=[0.1×0.1]12

Mass of Na2CO3.xH2O=[0.1×0.1]12×[106+18x]=1.43

⇒     [106+18x=286

18 x=180

x=10

subject: Chemistry
q:
Concentrated nitric acid is labelled as $75 \%$ by mass. The volume in mL of the solution which contains 30 g of nitric acid is 
____.
Given : Density of nitric acid solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$.
question:
Concentrated nitric acid is labelled as $75 \%$ by mass. The volume in mL of the solution which contains 30 g of nitric acid is 
____.
Given : Density of nitric acid solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$.
solution:
$75 \%$ by mass means :
$\because \quad 75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ in 100 g solution.
$\therefore \quad 30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ in $\frac{100}{75} \times 30 \mathrm{~g}$ solution.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{m}_{\text {solution }}=\frac{100 \times 30}{75} \mathrm{~g} \\
\because \quad & \mathrm{~V}_{\text {sol }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\text {sol }}}{\mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }}}=\frac{100 \times 30}{75 \times 1.25}=32 \mathrm{~mL}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Complete combustion of 3 g of ethane gives x×1022molecules of water. The value of x is ______ (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
Use :NA=6.023×1023; Atomic masses in u: C: 12.0 ; O: 16.0 ; H: 1.0
question:
Complete combustion of 3 g of ethane gives x×1022molecules of water. The value of x is ______ (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
Use :NA=6.023×1023; Atomic masses in u: C: 12.0 ; O: 16.0 ; H: 1.0
solution:
C2H6+ 72 O2→2CO2 + 3H2O

MWC2H6=30g

30g of C2H6  gives rise to 3 moles of H2O

3g of C2H6 gives rise to 0.3 moles of H2O

No. of molecules =0.3×6.023×1023
=18.069×1022

subject: Chemistry
q: <math><mn>1</mn></math> gram of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math><mn>0.01186</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: <math><mn>1</mn></math> gram of a carbonate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> on treatment with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> produces <math><mn>0.01186</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> . The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
M2CO3+2HCl→2MCl+H2O+CO2

From reaction,

Moles of M2CO3=Moles of CO2

Weight of M2CO3Molar Mass of M2CO3 =Weight of CO2Molar mass of CO2

1Molar Mass of M2CO3 =0.01186 ×4444

 1Molar Mass of M2CO3=0.01186

Molar Mass  =10.01186=84.3 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: A sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NaClO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is converted by heat to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> with a loss of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math>. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) obtained will be: (Given: Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>143</mn><mo>.5</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NaClO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is converted by heat to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> with a loss of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math>. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) obtained will be: (Given: Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>143</mn><mo>.5</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
2NaClO3→∆2NaCl+3O2
2 moles of NaCl=3 moles of oxygen
nNaCl=0.1632×23=1200×23=1300
The number of moles of NaCl=The number of moles of AgCl
1300=nAgCl
Weight of AgCl=1300×108+35.5=1300×143.5
=0.48 g

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The number of moles =MassMolar mass

The number of molecules = moles×NA

One mole of any gas occupies 22.7 L at STP.

(A) Moles of CH4=1616=1
Molecules =6.02×1023
Electrons =10×6.02×1023
=60.2×1023

(B) Moles of H2=12=0.5 mole
Volume at STP =12×22.7=11.35 lit .

(C) 1 mole N2=28gm

(D) 0.5 mole SO2=12×64=32gm

subject: Chemistry
q: A transition metal $M$ forms a volatile chloride which has a vapour density of $94.8$. If it contains $74.75 \%$ of chlorine the formula of the metal chloride will be
question: A transition metal $M$ forms a volatile chloride which has a vapour density of $94.8$. If it contains $74.75 \%$ of chlorine the formula of the metal chloride will be
solution:
$74.75 \%$ of chlorine means $74.75 \mathrm{~g}$ chlorine is present in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal chloride.
Weight of metal $=100 \mathrm{~g}-74.75 \mathrm{~g}$ $=25.25 \mathrm{~g}$
Equivalent weight
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\text { weight of metal }}{\text { weight of chlorine }} \times 35.5 \\
& =\frac{25.25}{74.75} \times 35.5=12
\end{aligned}
$$
Valency of metal
$$
\begin{array}{r}
=\frac{2 \times \text { V.D. }}{\text { Equivalent wt. of metal }+35.5} \\
=\frac{2 \times 94.8}{12+35.5}=4
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Formula of compound $=\mathrm{MCl}_4$
